walkthrough.title: Open the Box and Sort the Deck
walkthrough.text: Ahoy, matey! Let's get Skull King ready to sail. Open the box and find the main deck of cards. You'll see numbered suit cards, special character cards, Escape cards, player aid cards, bid reminder cards, and a scorepad. There may also be some blank cards and advanced variant cards (Loot, Kraken, White Whale) -- set those aside for now.

walkthrough.title: Remove Advanced and Blank Cards
walkthrough.text: For your first voyage, we're playing the basic game. Find and remove these cards from the deck: any Blank cards, the two Loot cards, the Kraken card, and the White Whale card. Set them back in the box. You won't need them until you've got a few games under your belt and are ready for rougher seas.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Fleet of Cards
walkthrough.text: Your deck should now contain: four suits of numbered cards (1-14 each) -- Parrots (green), Treasure Maps (purple), Treasure Chests (yellow), and the mighty Jolly Roger (black, which is the trump suit). Plus 5 Escape cards, 5 Pirate cards, 1 Tigress (she's a pirate who can also escape), 1 Skull King, and 2 Mermaids. That's 66 cards total for the basic game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Aids and Bid Reminders
walkthrough.text: Give each player a player aid card -- this handy reference shows the card hierarchy and scoring rules. Also hand each player a bid reminder card. These little cards are used during the bidding phase so everyone can remember what they bid. Keep the scorepad and a pen nearby for the designated scorekeeper.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Dealer and Shuffle
walkthrough.text: Pick a dealer however ye like -- youngest player, most pirate-looking player, or just volunteer. The dealer shuffles the entire deck thoroughly. In Skull King, the deal rotates clockwise each round, so everyone will take a turn at the helm.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Here's the key to Skull King's clever design: the game is played over exactly 10 rounds. In Round 1, each player gets just 1 card. In Round 2, each player gets 2 cards. This keeps climbing until Round 10, where each player has a full hand of 10 cards. The early rounds are quick skirmishes; the later rounds are full naval battles. After dealing, the remaining cards go face-down -- they're not used this round.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Scorepad
walkthrough.text: Write each player's name across the top of the scorepad. There are rows for each of the 10 rounds with spaces for both the bid and the score. Scores are cumulative -- you'll add (or subtract!) each round's points to the running total. The scallywag with the highest score after Round 10 claims the treasure and wins! Now deal Round 1 -- one card to each player -- and let's learn to bid.

Separate blank cards and advanced variant cards

Set these aside in the box for basic game

5 Escapes, 5 Pirates, 1 Tigress, 1 Skull King, 2 Mermaids
66 cards total in basic game

summary.title: Hand Out Player Aids and Bid Reminders
bullets: Each player gets 1 player aid card (hierarchy + scoring reference)
Each player gets 1 bid reminder card
Designate a scorekeeper with scorepad and pen

summary.title: Choose a Dealer and Shuffle
bullets: Pick any player as first dealer
Dealer shuffles entire deck thoroughly
Deal rotates clockwise each round

summary.title: Understand the Round Structure
bullets: 10 rounds total: Round N = N cards dealt to each player
Round 1 = 1 card, Round 10 = 10 cards
Remaining cards are set aside unused each round

summary.title: Set Up the Scorepad
bullets: Write player names across top, rows for 10 rounds
Track bid and score each round; scores are cumulative
Highest total after Round 10 wins

walkthrough.title: Dealing Each Round
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, the dealer shuffles the entire deck and deals cards equal to the round number to each player. Round 1? One card each. Round 5? Five cards each. Place any remaining cards face-down in the center -- they're out of play for this round. After dealing, players pick up their hand and study it secretly.

walkthrough.title: The Bidding Phase -- Yo Ho Ho!
walkthrough.text: Now comes the most exciting part of Skull King: the bid! Every player looks at their hand and decides exactly how many tricks they think they'll win this round. Then all players make a fist over the table. Together, everyone counts 'Yo! Ho! Ho!' -- pounding their fist on each word. On the third pound, all players simultaneously reveal their bid by extending that many fingers. A closed fist means zero -- ye plan to dodge every trick. The scorekeeper records all bids.

walkthrough.title: Playing Tricks -- The Basics
walkthrough.text: The player to the left of the dealer leads the first trick by playing one card face-up. Then each player clockwise plays one card. After everyone has played, the highest card wins the trick, and the winner leads the next one. This continues until all cards in hand are played -- that's one trick per card dealt this round.

walkthrough.title: Following Suit
walkthrough.text: When a numbered suit card is led, you must follow suit if you have a card of that suit in your hand. If you don't have that suit, you may play any card. Important: this rule only applies to numbered suit cards! Special cards (Escapes, Pirates, Mermaids, Skull King, Tigress) can always be played regardless of what was led, even if you have cards of the led suit.

walkthrough.title: Card Hierarchy -- Who Wins the Trick?
walkthrough.text: Here's the pecking order on the high seas. Escape cards always lose -- they're the lowest. Next come numbered suit cards (1-14), where higher numbers beat lower ones. The Jolly Roger suit (black) is trump and beats all other suit cards regardless of number. Above all numbered cards sit the Pirates -- they beat every numbered card. The Skull King is the most fearsome, beating all Pirates. But even the Skull King has a weakness: Mermaids lure the Skull King to his doom! If multiple cards of the same rank are played (like two Pirates), the first one played wins.

walkthrough.title: The Mermaid-Pirate-Skull King Triangle
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of Skull King's drama. Pirates beat all numbered cards and Mermaids. The Skull King beats all Pirates. Mermaids beat the Skull King -- but lose to Pirates and numbered cards (unless only Escapes and Mermaids are in the trick). There's one special scenario: if a Pirate, the Skull King, and a Mermaid are all played in the same trick, the Mermaid wins! She enchants the Skull King even in a crowded battle.

walkthrough.title: Leading with Special Cards
walkthrough.text: When an Escape is led, it doesn't set a suit -- the next player who plays a numbered suit card determines the suit for that trick. When a character card (Pirate, Mermaid, Skull King, or Tigress as Pirate) is led, there is no suit to follow for that trick -- everyone may play whatever they wish. This opens the door for all sorts of tactical plays.

walkthrough.title: The Tigress -- A Pirate with Options
walkthrough.text: The Tigress is a unique card. When you play her, you choose: play her face-up as a Pirate (she fights like any other Pirate), or play her face-down as an Escape (she slinks away from the battle). You don't reveal a face-down Tigress until the trick is over. This flexibility makes her one of the most versatile cards in the game.

walkthrough.title: Scoring -- The Skull King's Method
walkthrough.text: After all tricks in a round are played, it's time to settle accounts. If you bid one or more and hit your bid exactly, you earn +20 points per trick you bid. If you missed your bid (over or under), you lose 10 points for each trick you were off. Example: bid 3, won 3 = +60 points. Bid 3, won 5 = off by 2 = -20 points. Bid 3, won 1 = off by 2 = -20 points.

walkthrough.title: Scoring a Zero Bid
walkthrough.text: Bidding zero is a bold gamble with its own rules. If you bid zero and successfully win no tricks, you earn +10 points multiplied by the number of cards dealt that round. So bidding zero in Round 7 and succeeding earns you a whopping +70 points! But if you win even one trick after bidding zero, you lose that same amount: -10 times the round number. In Round 7, that's -70 points. The stakes for a zero bid grow fiercer as the game goes on.

walkthrough.title: Bonus Points -- 14 Cards and Character Captures
walkthrough.text: If -- and only if -- you hit your bid exactly, you can earn bonus points on top of your bid score. For each 14-value card you captured in your tricks, you earn +10 points (or +20 for the black Jolly Roger 14). For character captures: if your Pirate captured a Mermaid, +20 bonus. If your Skull King captured a Pirate, +30 bonus per Pirate taken. If your Mermaid captured the Skull King, +40 bonus. These bonuses can be massive and are the key to running away with the game.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Winning
walkthrough.text: After scoring Round 10, add up each player's cumulative score across all 10 rounds. The pirate captain with the highest total score wins! Scores can swing wildly -- a player deep in the negative after Round 5 can storm back with big late-round plays and bonuses. Never count anyone out until the final trick is played. If there's a tie, the player with the highest single-round score breaks it.

walkthrough.title: Welcome Aboard, Crew!
walkthrough.text: Let's sail through a practice game with four players: Captain Morgan, Blackbeard, Anne Bonny, and Calico Jack. We'll play through the first few rounds to learn the ropes, then jump ahead to see how the later rounds work. Morgan is our first dealer. Remember: Round 1 means 1 card each!

walkthrough.title: Round 1 -- Dealing and Bidding
walkthrough.text: Morgan shuffles the full deck and deals one card to each player. Blackbeard gets the Jolly Roger 10 (black 10). Anne Bonny gets an Escape. Calico Jack gets a Parrot 7 (green 7). Morgan gets a Pirate. With just one card, bidding is straightforward. 'Yo! Ho! Ho!' -- Morgan bids 1 (she has a Pirate, which beats almost everything). Blackbeard bids 1 (black trump is strong). Anne bids 0 (Escape always loses). Calico bids 0 (a middling green card with no trump -- risky to bid 1).

walkthrough.title: Round 1 -- Playing the Trick
walkthrough.text: Blackbeard sits to Morgan's left, so he leads. He plays his Jolly Roger 10. Anne plays her Escape (it always loses, perfect for her zero bid). Calico plays his Parrot 7 -- no black cards, so he plays what he has. Morgan plays her Pirate. The Pirate beats all numbered cards, so Morgan wins the trick! She got the 1 trick she bid.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 -- Scoring
walkthrough.text: Time to tally up! Morgan bid 1 and won 1 -- that's +20 points. Blackbeard bid 1 but won 0 -- off by 1, so -10 points. Anne bid 0 and won 0 in Round 1 -- success! That's +10 x 1 = +10 points. Calico bid 0 and won 0 -- also +10 points. The scorekeeper writes it down: Morgan 20, Blackbeard -10, Anne 10, Calico 10.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 -- A Bigger Hand
walkthrough.text: The deal passes to Blackbeard. He shuffles the entire deck and deals 2 cards each. Anne Bonny gets a Treasure Map 12 (purple 12) and a Treasure Chest 3 (yellow 3). She thinks: 'That 12 is pretty high, I could win one trick.' She plans to bid 1. This is where strategy starts to emerge -- with 2 cards, you need to think about which tricks to fight for and which to dodge.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 -- Bidding and Playing
walkthrough.text: 'Yo! Ho! Ho!' -- Morgan bids 1, Blackbeard bids 0, Anne bids 1, Calico bids 1. That's 3 tricks bid but only 2 tricks available (Round 2 = 2 tricks). At least one player is going to miss! This is totally normal in Skull King -- the bids don't have to add up to the number of tricks. Calico leads trick 1 with a Parrot 9. Anne has no green cards, so she plays her Treasure Chest 3 (can't follow suit, but it won't win). Morgan plays Parrot 5 (must follow green). Blackbeard plays an Escape. Calico's 9 wins. Trick 2: Calico leads Jolly Roger 6. Morgan plays Treasure Map 8 (can't follow black). Anne plays Treasure Map 12 (can't follow black). Blackbeard plays an Escape. Jolly Roger 6 is black trump -- it beats all non-black suits, so Calico wins again!

walkthrough.title: Round 2 -- Scoring and a Mistake to Learn From
walkthrough.text: Calico bid 1 but won 2 -- off by 1, so -10 points. Ouch! This is Mistake #1: overbidding (or underbidding) because you didn't account for trump. Calico's Jolly Roger 6 was stronger than he realized -- any black card beats all non-black cards, even high ones. Morgan bid 1, won 0 = -10. Anne bid 1, won 0 = -10. Blackbeard bid 0, won 0 in Round 2 = +20. Running scores: Morgan 10, Blackbeard 10, Anne 0, Calico 0.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 -- More Cards, More Decisions
walkthrough.text: Anne deals 3 cards each. Morgan gets Skull King, Escape, and Treasure Chest 4. She's thrilled -- the Skull King beats almost everything! She bids 1, planning to win with the Skull King and escape the other tricks. Blackbeard has Pirate, Parrot 14, Jolly Roger 3. He bids 2. Anne has Mermaid, Treasure Map 9, Parrot 2. She bids 1, hoping the Mermaid will catch something good. Calico has Jolly Roger 11, Treasure Chest 8, Escape. He bids 1.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 -- Trick 1: The Skull King Strikes
walkthrough.text: Calico leads the Jolly Roger 11 (strong trump). Anne plays Treasure Map 9 (can't follow black). Morgan drops the Skull King -- the king himself! Blackbeard sees the Skull King and plays his Escape rather than waste his Pirate (smart move, saving it for later). The Skull King crushes everything. Morgan wins the trick and has already met her bid of 1.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 -- Trick 2: A Pirate's Plunder
walkthrough.text: Morgan leads with her Escape (she already got her 1 trick and wants to dodge the rest). Blackbeard plays his Pirate! Anne, seeing the Pirate, considers her options. She plays her Parrot 2 to dump a weak card -- no point wasting the Mermaid here since a Pirate beats Mermaids. Calico plays Treasure Chest 8. Blackbeard's Pirate wins the trick. That's 1 for Blackbeard.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 -- Trick 3: Mermaid Misfire
walkthrough.text: Blackbeard leads with Parrot 14 (the highest green card!). Anne must follow green if she can, but she has no green -- she plays her Mermaid. Calico has no green, plays his Escape. Morgan plays Treasure Chest 4 (no green either). Now, who wins? The Mermaid only beats the Skull King -- against regular numbered cards, she loses. The Parrot 14 wins because the Mermaid has no Skull King to enchant. This is Mistake #2: Anne played her Mermaid hoping it would win, but Mermaids only triumph over the Skull King. Without the Skull King in the trick, the Mermaid is just a fancy Escape. Anne wins 0 tricks.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 -- Scoring with Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Morgan bid 1, won 1 = +20. Plus she captured the Jolly Roger 11 in her Skull King trick... wait, the 11 isn't a 14, so no bonus for that. Just +20. Blackbeard bid 2, won 2 = +40. And he captured the Parrot 14! Since he hit his bid, he earns the 14 bonus: +10 for a standard suit 14. Total: +50 this round. Anne bid 1, won 0 = -10. No bonuses since she missed her bid. Calico bid 1, won 0 = -10. Running scores: Morgan 30, Blackbeard 60, Anne -10, Calico -10.

walkthrough.title: Fast Forward to Round 6 -- The Stakes Rise
walkthrough.text: Let's jump ahead. It's Round 6, so each player has 6 cards. The running scores are Morgan 80, Blackbeard 110, Anne 40, Calico 20. With more cards in hand, bidding becomes much harder -- and more rewarding. A correct bid of 4 in Round 6 is worth +80 points. But zero bids are now worth +/-60 points. The tension ratchets up every round!

walkthrough.title: Round 6 -- The Overbid Trap
walkthrough.text: Calico looks at his hand: Pirate, Jolly Roger 13, Jolly Roger 8, Treasure Chest 12, Parrot 6, Escape. He sees the Pirate and two black trump cards and thinks 'I'll win at least 3 tricks, maybe 4!' He bids 4. This is Mistake #3: overbidding because of strong-looking cards without considering what others might have. Pirates and Skull King roaming around can swallow your trump cards. And in a 4-player game with 6 cards each, that's 24 of 66 cards in play -- there's a lot of firepower out there.

walkthrough.title: Round 6 -- The Pain of Missing by Two
walkthrough.text: After the round plays out, Calico's Pirate and Jolly Roger 13 win tricks, but his Jolly Roger 8 gets trumped by Morgan's Jolly Roger 12, and his other cards fall flat. He wins only 2 tricks on a bid of 4 -- off by 2, costing him -20 points. If he'd bid 2 or 3, he would have been much closer. The lesson: in the middle rounds, conservative bids are often safer than aggressive ones. You can always aim higher once you've read the table better.

walkthrough.title: Round 8 -- Tracking What's Been Played
walkthrough.text: Now we're in Round 8 with 8 cards each. Blackbeard has been quietly paying attention to which cards have come out. He noticed that 3 of the 5 Pirates have already been played, the Skull King showed up in Round 6, and both Mermaids appeared in Round 7. With this knowledge, his remaining Pirate is extremely safe -- nothing currently in the deck can beat it except the other unplayed Pirate (if played first). Players who track what's been played have a huge advantage in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: Wait -- Reshuffle Every Round!
walkthrough.text: Hold on -- we need to correct something important! In Skull King, the entire deck is reshuffled between every round. So tracking cards from previous rounds doesn't help -- every round is a fresh deal from the full 66-card deck. What IS valuable is tracking cards within the current round: which trumps, Pirates, and character cards have appeared in earlier tricks of the same round. In Round 8 with 8 tricks, that's a lot of information to absorb. Blackbeard should track within the round, not across rounds!

walkthrough.title: Round 10 -- The Grand Finale
walkthrough.text: It's the final round! Each player holds 10 cards from a full deal. Scores going in: Blackbeard 210, Morgan 180, Anne 130, Calico 60. Everything is still up for grabs -- a perfect bid of 5 in Round 10 is worth 100 points, plus potential bonus points. Anne has a hand with the Skull King, two Pirates, and the Jolly Roger 14. She counts her likely tricks carefully and bids 5.

walkthrough.title: Round 10 -- Bonus Bonanza
walkthrough.text: The round plays out and Anne nails her bid of 5 exactly! Let's add it up. Base score: 5 tricks x 20 = +100 points. Her Skull King captured two Pirates during the round, earning +30 bonus per Pirate = +60. She also took the Jolly Roger 14 in one of her tricks: +20 bonus (it's the black 14). Anne's total for Round 10: 100 + 60 + 20 = +180 points! Her final score leaps from 130 to 310, overtaking Blackbeard!

walkthrough.title: Final Scores and the Power of Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Final scores: Anne 310, Blackbeard 250, Morgan 220, Calico 90. Anne's massive Round 10 with character capture bonuses vaulted her from third place to first! This shows why bonus points matter so much in Skull King. A Pirate capturing a Mermaid (+20), the Skull King capturing Pirates (+30 each), and a Mermaid capturing the Skull King (+40) can completely change the outcome -- but only if you hit your bid exactly.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaway: Bid Carefully
walkthrough.text: The biggest lesson from our practice game: your bid is everything. Getting your bid right earns points and unlocks bonuses. Getting it wrong costs points and wastes any bonuses you might have earned. In early rounds (1-3 cards), bids are simple. In middle rounds (4-6), bid conservatively until you learn to read the table. In late rounds (7-10), use what you've seen in the current round's tricks to adjust your play.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaway: Respect the Hierarchy
walkthrough.text: Remember the rock-paper-scissors of the character cards: Pirates beat numbered cards (and Mermaids), Skull King beats Pirates, Mermaids beat Skull King. Don't play your Mermaid hoping to win a trick full of numbered cards -- she'll lose. Don't assume your high trump will survive if Pirates or the Skull King are lurking. And always remember: Escapes are your secret weapon for ducking unwanted tricks.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaway: Zero Bids Are Powerful but Dangerous
walkthrough.text: Bidding zero is a high-risk, high-reward strategy that scales with the round number. In Round 3, a successful zero bid is only +30 points. In Round 8, it's +80. But failure costs the same amount in the negative. If your hand is full of low numbered cards and Escapes, a zero bid can be brilliant. If you have even one card that might accidentally win a trick, think twice.

walkthrough.title: You're Ready to Set Sail!
walkthrough.text:
You've now seen dealing, bidding, trick-taking, the card hierarchy, scoring with bonuses, and the most common mistakes new players make. You're ready for your first real game of Skull King!

Before you go, check out the General Tips section for more pirate wisdom, and the Appendix for a quick-reference glossary of every card and term. And remember -- your GM Guide is always here. Just tap the Q&A tab to ask any question during your game. Fair winds and following seas, Captain!

bullets: Scales with round number: Round 8 zero = +/-80 points

summary.title: You're Ready to Set Sail!
bullets: Check General Tips for strategy, Appendix for card reference
Ask your GM Guide (Q&A tab) any question during play

walkthrough.title: Start Conservative, Then Calibrate
walkthrough.text: In your first few games, bid on the low side. It's better to underbid by one trick and lose 10 points than to overbid by two and lose 20. As you get a feel for which cards win tricks and how other players bid, you'll naturally get more accurate. Skull King rewards experience -- every game sharpens your instincts.

walkthrough.title: Escapes Are Your Best Friends
walkthrough.text: New players often undervalue Escape cards. They're guaranteed losers, which makes them perfect for controlling your trick count. If you've already won the tricks you need, Escapes let you safely duck out of the remaining ones. The Tigress played face-down is essentially a sixth Escape. Treasure these cards -- they protect your bid.

walkthrough.title: Pay Attention to Everyone's Bids
walkthrough.text: The moment bids are revealed, do some quick math. If you're in a 4-player game in Round 5 (5 tricks total) and the bids add up to 7, that means at least 2 tricks worth of overbidding will happen. Some players will definitely miss. If bids add up to less than the available tricks, someone is guaranteed to win more than they wanted. Use this info to adjust your play.

walkthrough.title: Lead with Purpose
walkthrough.text: When you lead a trick, you set the tone. Lead with a strong card when you want to win. Lead with an Escape when you want to hand control to someone else. Lead with a character card when you want to create chaos (since no suit is set, everyone can play anything). Every lead is a decision -- don't just play the first card you see.

walkthrough.title: Hunt for Bonus Points
walkthrough.text: Bonus points are how good players separate from great ones. If you have the Skull King and see Pirates being played, try to capture them (+30 each!). If you have a Pirate, look for opportunities to snag a Mermaid (+20). And always try to collect 14-value cards in your winning tricks. But remember: bonuses only count if you nail your bid. Don't chase bonuses at the cost of accuracy.

walkthrough.title: The Zero Bid Sweet Spot
walkthrough.text: Bidding zero is strongest in rounds 6-10 when the payoff is 60-100 points. The ideal zero-bid hand has lots of low numbered cards, Escapes, and no trump or character cards. If your hand has even one dangerous card (a Pirate, a high Jolly Roger, or a 14), think twice -- one slip and you're deep in the red. In early rounds, zero bids are low-value (+10 to +30), so don't be afraid to bid 1 instead.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Power Cards
walkthrough.text: Don't rush to play your strongest cards. In longer rounds, save your Pirates and trump cards for when you know they'll secure a trick you need. Play weaker cards early to see what others are holding. If you play your Pirate on the first trick of Round 8, you might win a trick full of low cards -- but you'll have 7 more tricks where you're vulnerable. Patience is a pirate's greatest virtue.

walkthrough.title: Have Fun with the Theme!
walkthrough.text: Skull King is a game about swashbuckling and high-seas drama. Don't just quietly play cards -- announce your Pirates with swagger, moan when the Skull King steals your trick, and cheer when your Mermaid enchants the King. The 'Yo! Ho! Ho!' bidding ritual is there for a reason: this game is meant to be loud, dramatic, and hilarious. Lean into it!

summary.title: Start Conservative, Then Calibrate
bullets: Underbid rather than overbid in early games
Losing 10 points (off by 1) is better than -20 (off by 2)

summary.title: Escapes Are Your Best Friends
bullets: Guaranteed losers = perfect for protecting your bid
Tigress face-down acts as a 6th Escape

summary.title: Pay Attention to Everyone's Bids
bullets: Add up all bids vs. available tricks
Overbid total = guaranteed misses; underbid total = forced extra wins

summary.title: Lead with Purpose
bullets: Strong card = win the trick; Escape = pass control
Character lead = no suit restriction; creates chaos

summary.title: Hunt for Bonus Points
bullets: Skull King capturing Pirates (+30 each), Pirate capturing Mermaid (+20), Mermaid capturing SK (+40)
14 cards: +10 standard, +20 black; only if bid is correct

summary.title: The Zero Bid Sweet Spot
bullets: Most valuable in rounds 6-10 (60-100 point swing)
Ideal hand: low cards + Escapes, no trump or characters

summary.title: Timing Your Power Cards
bullets: Save Pirates and high trumps for crucial tricks
Play weaker cards early to scout others' hands

summary.title: Have Fun with the Theme!
bullets: Lean into the pirate theme -- swagger, drama, 'Yo Ho Ho!'
The game is meant to be loud, dramatic, and hilarious

walkthrough.title: Bid-Counting Math at the Table
walkthrough.text: When bids are revealed, immediately calculate the 'overbid gap' or 'underbid gap.' In a 4-player Round 7 game with 7 tricks, if total bids are 10, there's an overbid of 3 -- meaning 3 tricks worth of bids won't be fulfilled. If you bid high, you're competing with everyone else. If you bid low or zero, you benefit from the chaos of others fighting for scarce tricks. Advanced players use this math to decide whether to play aggressively or defensively.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Trick Lead
walkthrough.text: Whoever leads a trick has enormous power. They choose the suit (or remove suit requirements by leading a character). If you need to win exactly 2 more tricks, winning the lead lets you set up favorable situations. Conversely, if you've hit your bid, deliberately lose the lead so someone else has to make tough decisions. Expert Skull King is all about controlling when you have the lead and when you don't.

walkthrough.title: The Skull King as Bait
walkthrough.text: The Skull King is the strongest card in the game -- but the +40 bonus for a Mermaid capturing it means experienced opponents will bait you into playing it early. If you have the Skull King, hold it until you're confident no Mermaids are waiting. If you have a Mermaid and suspect someone has the Skull King, try to maneuver them into playing it while you still have the Mermaid in hand. This cat-and-mouse game is the deepest layer of Skull King strategy.

walkthrough.title: Suit Starvation Strategy
walkthrough.text: If your hand is missing one or more suits, you have hidden power. When that suit is led, you're free to play anything -- trump, a Pirate, an Escape. Players with 'holes' in their suit distribution can play more flexibly. When bidding, assess not just your high cards but which suits you're void in. Being void in a common suit is almost as good as having extra trump cards.

walkthrough.title: Manipulating Other Players' Bids
walkthrough.text: In later games, you'll notice patterns: aggressive bidders, cautious bidders, and zero-bid specialists. If a player always bids zero in late rounds, lead with low cards in their weak suits to try to force them into winning a trick. If someone overbids, you can sometimes 'steal' their expected tricks by trumping in unexpectedly. Reading the table is what separates casual players from Skull King captains.

walkthrough.title: The Mid-Round Pivot
walkthrough.text: Partway through a round, you may realize you can't hit your bid. If you bid 3 and have won 0 tricks with 2 remaining, you can't hit 3 -- so pivot to minimize damage. Being off by 1 (-10) is much better than off by 3 (-30). Play to get as close to your bid as possible. Some experts even sacrifice bonus opportunities to get within 1 of their bid rather than risk being further off.

walkthrough.title: Counting Cards Within a Round
walkthrough.text: In Round 10, with 10 tricks played sequentially, a huge amount of information is revealed. Track which Pirates have appeared, whether the Skull King has been played, how many Jolly Roger (black) cards are gone, and how many 14s are still live. By trick 6 or 7, a sharp player knows most of what's left and can play their final cards with near-certainty. This skill takes practice but dramatically improves your accuracy.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Character Capture Bonuses
walkthrough.text: The biggest single-play bonus is Mermaid capturing Skull King (+40). If you have a Mermaid and someone has bid aggressively (suggesting they might hold the Skull King), hold your Mermaid as long as possible. The Skull King holder will eventually need to play it to win a trick. That's when you strike. Similarly, if you hold the Skull King and see two Pirates in others' hands (from their aggressive bids), you could potentially capture both for +60 in bonus.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame -- Rounds 8, 9, and 10
walkthrough.text: The final three rounds often determine the winner. A correct bid of 6 in Round 10 with bonus captures can yield 150+ points in a single round. Going into these rounds behind, you need to bid aggressively and hunt bonuses. Going in ahead, you can play safe with moderate bids or even zero bids to lock in your lead. Always calculate: what score do I need, and what's the safest way to get there?

walkthrough.title: Advanced Variant -- Adding Kraken, Loot, and White Whale
walkthrough.text: Once you've mastered the base game, add the advanced cards for even more chaos. The Kraken destroys the entire trick -- nobody wins it, which devastates players who needed that trick for their bid. The Loot cards let the winner of a trick draw extra cards, creating information asymmetry. The White Whale provides a massive bonus target but changes trick dynamics. These cards add another layer of unpredictability and are recommended after 3-5 games with the base set.

summary.title: Bid-Counting Math
bullets: Calculate overbid/underbid gap when bids are revealed
High overbid = chaos; benefits conservative bidders

summary.title: Controlling the Trick Lead
bullets: Leading lets you set the suit and control the trick
After hitting your bid, lose the lead intentionally

summary.title: The Skull King as Bait
bullets: Hold Skull King until Mermaids are accounted for
Hold Mermaid to ambush a Skull King player; +40 bonus

summary.title: Suit Starvation Strategy
bullets: Being void in a suit = freedom to trump or play specials
Assess suit voids when bidding, not just high cards

summary.title: Manipulating Other Players' Bids
bullets: Force zero-bidders to win by leading low in their weak suits
Trump into overbidders' expected tricks to steal them

summary.title: The Mid-Round Pivot
bullets: If bid is unreachable, minimize the gap (off by 1 > off by 3)
Sacrifice bonus chances to reduce penalty

summary.title: Counting Cards Within a Round
bullets: Track Pirates, Skull King, black trumps, and 14s as tricks play out
By trick 6-7 of Round 10, most key cards are known

summary.title: Maximizing Character Capture Bonuses
bullets: Hold Mermaid for Skull King ambush (+40)
Skull King vs. multiple Pirates = +30 each

summary.title: The Endgame -- Rounds 8, 9, 10
bullets: Final rounds determine winners; single round can yield 150+ points
Behind? Bid aggressively + hunt bonuses. Ahead? Play safe or bid zero

summary.title: Advanced Variant Cards
bullets: Kraken: destroys entire trick (nobody wins)
Loot: winner draws extra cards; White Whale: massive bonus target
Add after 3-5 base games

entries.definition: One of four numbered suits, cards 1-14. Green colored with a parrot icon. No special power -- standard suit.
One of four numbered suits, cards 1-14. Purple colored with a treasure map icon. No special power -- standard suit.
One of four numbered suits, cards 1-14. Yellow colored with a treasure chest icon. No special power -- standard suit.
The fourth numbered suit, cards 1-14. Black colored with a skull-and-crossbones. This is the trump suit -- any Jolly Roger card beats any card from the other three suits, regardless of number.
There are 5 Escape cards in the deck. An Escape always loses the trick. It can be played at any time regardless of the suit led. Perfect for avoiding unwanted tricks.

There are 5 Pirate cards. Pirates beat all numbered suit cards (including Jolly Roger/trump). They lose to the Skull King. If multiple Pirates are in a trick, the first one played wins. Pirates also beat Mermaids.
The single most powerful card. Beats all Pirates and all numbered cards. Only defeated by a Mermaid. If the Skull King captures Pirates, the player earns +30 bonus per Pirate (if bid was correct).

There are 2 Mermaid cards. Mermaids beat the Skull King (earning a +40 bonus if bid is correct) but lose to Pirates and numbered cards. If Pirate + Skull King + Mermaid are all in one trick, the Mermaid wins.

A unique dual-purpose card. Played face-up, she acts as a Pirate. Played face-down, she acts as an Escape. Her identity is revealed when the trick resolves. Counts as a Pirate for all purposes when played face-up.

The highest numbered card in each suit. When captured in a winning trick, earns +10 bonus (standard suits) or +20 bonus (Jolly Roger/black 14). Bonus only applies if the capturing player hit their bid.
Advanced variant card (2 in deck). When played, the winner of the trick draws bonus cards. Adds information asymmetry. Remove for basic game.
Advanced variant card. When played, it destroys the entire trick -- nobody wins it. Devastating for players who needed that trick. Remove for basic game.
Advanced variant card. Provides a massive bonus target that changes trick dynamics. Remove for basic game.
The Jolly Roger (black) suit is always trump. Any black numbered card beats any non-black numbered card, regardless of the numbers on the cards. A Jolly Roger 1 beats a Parrot 14.

When a numbered suit card is led, you must play a card of the same suit if you have one. If you don't have that suit, you may play any card. Special cards (Escape, Pirate, Mermaid, Skull King, Tigress) are exempt from this rule.
One round of card play where each player contributes one card. The highest-ranking card wins the trick, and that player collects the cards and leads the next trick.

Playing the first card in a trick. The leader sets the suit (if a numbered card) or creates a suit-free trick (if a character card or Escape). The player left of the dealer leads trick 1; subsequent tricks are led by the previous winner.
The number of tricks a player predicts they will win in the current round. Revealed simultaneously on 'Yo! Ho! Ho!' using extended fingers (fist = zero). Accuracy determines scoring.
One complete cycle of dealing, bidding, playing all tricks, and scoring. The game has 10 rounds. Round N deals N cards to each player.
Non-numbered cards: Escape, Pirate, Skull King, Mermaid, and Tigress. These can be played at any time regardless of the suit led. They follow their own hierarchy instead of suit rules.
If you bid 1 or more and hit exactly: +20 points per trick bid. If you miss: -10 points per trick you were off (over or under).
If you bid zero and win no tricks: +10 x the round number. If you win any trick: -10 x the round number. Example: Round 7 zero bid success = +70, failure = -70.
Awarded only if you hit your bid. +10 points for each standard suit 14 (green, purple, yellow) you captured in your tricks. +20 points for the Jolly Roger (black) 14.
When the Skull King wins a trick containing one or more Pirates, the Skull King's player earns +30 per Pirate captured. Only awarded if the player hit their bid exactly.
When a Pirate wins a trick containing a Mermaid, the Pirate's player earns +20 bonus. Only awarded if the player hit their bid exactly.
When a Mermaid defeats the Skull King, the Mermaid's player earns +40 bonus. The ultimate character capture bonus. Only awarded if the player hit their bid exactly.

An alternative scoring method where all players have the same point potential each round (10 x cards dealt). Direct hit = full points, off by 1 = half points, off by 2 or more = zero. Simpler but removes bonus incentives.
Bidding more tricks than you actually win. Penalized at -10 per trick off. Common mistake with strong-looking hands that face unexpected competition from trump, Pirates, or the Skull King.
Bidding fewer tricks than you actually win. Same penalty as overbidding: -10 per trick off. Happens when your cards are stronger than expected or opponents play Escapes to dodge tricks.
A reference card given to each player showing the card hierarchy and scoring summary. Keep it visible during play -- especially helpful for new players.
A small card each player uses to remember their bid throughout the round. The scorekeeper also records bids on the scorepad.
Used to track bids and cumulative scores across all 10 rounds. Has columns for each player and rows for each round with spaces for bid and score.
The dealer changes each round, rotating clockwise. The player to the left of the dealer always leads the first trick of each round.

The bidding chant. All players make a fist and pound the table on 'Yo!', 'Ho!', 'Ho!' -- then simultaneously reveal their bid with extended fingers. This ensures all bids are made independently and simultaneously.
A trick where no suit needs to be followed. This happens when the lead card is a character card (Pirate, Mermaid, Skull King, or Tigress as Pirate). All players may play any card from their hand.

The special scenario where all three character types appear in one trick. In this case, the Mermaid wins the trick, regardless of play order. She enchants the Skull King even in the presence of Pirates.
